runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 5 in a U.S. tax-benefit microsimulation model
citations: Saez, Slemrod, and Giertz (2012) - The elasticity of taxable income with respect to marginal tax rates
Chetty (2012) - Bounds on elasticities with optimization frictions
Keane and Rogerson (2012) - Micro and macro labor supply elasticities
runs.reasoningSummary: The substitution elasticity for mid-decile earners is typically estimated at 0.2–0.3 in behavioral microsimulation contexts. Decile 5 (median earnings) represents middle-income workers with moderate wage growth and tax sensitivity. I estimate around 0.25 as a central value, with substantial uncertainty reflecting variation across estimation methods, time periods, and model specifications. The distribution is right-skewed given heterogeneity and the difficulty of pinpointing behavioral responses precisely in simulation settings.

runs.reasoningSummary: Middle-decile earners typically exhibit modest substitution elasticities relative to higher earners. Uncertainty is substantial given heterogeneity and specification sensitivity in simulation contexts.
